one_line_takeaway: BadCM hides backdoor triggers inside CLIP-identified modality-invariant image regions and keywords, yielding the first unified invisible cross-modal backdoor that matches visible attacks' potency while strongly evading five adapted defenses -- at the cost of somewhat lower ASR than visible baselines in text-side and dual-key settings.
key_finding: BadCM achieves attack effectiveness comparable to VISIBLE attacks while being far stealthier and markedly more defense-resistant. In V2L (Table I) it beats the SOTA invisible attack FTrojan on ASR across all three datasets and posts the best SSIM/MSE (e.g. MS-COCO ASR 71.62 vs benign 88.07 BA, SSIM 0.980, MSE 0.18). Its stealth advantage translates into strong defense evasion: after SPECTRE at a 40% poisoning ratio, 45.3% of BadCM poison remained vs 25.0% for BadNets; after DB-R, BadCM ASR stayed 72.28% vs BadNets 9.79%; after Februus, 71.22% vs 20.99%; under Fine-Pruning BadCM held >70% ASR at 85% neuron pruning; and after the textual ONION defense BadCM recovered to 36.05% ASR vs BadNets 11.16%. The framework generalizes to dual-key VQA with average ASR 82-87% across five architectures with essentially unchanged clean accuracy.
core_contribution: BadCM is presented as the first unified, invisible data-poisoning backdoor framework for image-text cross-modal learning. Its distinctive contributions (not the cited prior work) are: (1) the concept of a 'bilateral' cross-modal backdoor, i.e. visual-to-linguistic (V2L, poison images -> mis-retrieve text) and linguistic-to-visual (L2V, poison text -> mis-retrieve images) attacks where only ONE modality is poisoned, positioned as complementary to the existing dual-key attack (DKMB [11]) which requires triggers in both modalities; (2) a 'cross-modal mining' scheme that uses a frozen pretrained vision-language surrogate (CLIP) to score and select 'modality-invariant components' (salient Faster-RCNN object regions for images, capped at <=30% of image area via dynamic programming; keywords for text, capped at <=40% of tokens) as the carrier region for the trigger; (3) two modality-specific stealthy trigger generators -- a UNet+PatchGAN adversarial generator that hides a reference patch trigger as imperceptible perturbations confined to the invariant image regions, and a BERT masked-LM greedy synonym-substitution algorithm (Algorithm 1) that hides a rare-word trigger by substituting invariant keywords with fluent synonyms. The single framework is shown to cover V2L, L2V, and dual-key attacks.
threat_model: Data-poisoning backdoor, black-box. The adversary is a malicious data provider (or someone publishing poisoned data online); victims collect poisoned samples, mix them with clean data, and train their own cross-modal model. The adversary can ONLY manipulate the training dataset (poisoning rate p%, default 5%) and has NO control over and no knowledge of the victim training process or the victim's feature extractors -- hence a pretrained VLM (CLIP) is used as a surrogate. Attack goals: (1) preserve benign accuracy so the compromise is not noticed, and (2) force retrieval of an adversary-chosen target label (retrieval) or emit a target answer (VQA) whenever the modality-specific trigger is present. Stealthiness against both human inspection and backdoor-detection defenses is an explicit objective. Real-world risks cited include compromised medical cross-modal retrieval and visual assistance for the blind.
limitations: The authors themselves flag several: (1) In L2V/textual attacks BadCM's raw ASR is LOWER than visible baselines (MS-COCO 64.96 vs BadNets 77.50; IAPR-TC 61.25 vs 74.98) -- stealth is traded for effectiveness; they attribute this partly to using only a simple greedy substitution and expect a heuristic optimizer to do better. (2) On dual-key VQA, DKMB reaches ~98.7% ASR vs BadCM's ~83-87% because DKMB uses visible triggers explicitly optimized for the object detectors, whereas BadCM stays invisible/black-box. (3) Effectiveness depends heavily on the surrogate: a weaker R50+LSTM surrogate drops ASR (V2L 70.03, L2V 58.99) vs CLIP (71.62/64.96), so a powerful VLM is needed. (4) ASR is sensitive to the chosen target label (driven by class-frequency imbalance) and positively correlated with victim model capacity. (5) The five defenses were not natively applicable to cross-modal tasks and had to be adapted by the authors, so defense-resistance claims are against author-adapted classification defenses rather than purpose-built cross-modal defenses.

datasets_used: NUS-WIDE (269,648 images, 81 concepts; 21 most-frequent-concept subset used) - cross-modal retrieval
MS-COCO (123,287 images, one of five captions paired, 80 labels) - cross-modal retrieval
IAPR-TC (20,000 image-text pairs, 255 categories) - cross-modal retrieval
quantitative_results: V2L (Table I) BadCM ASR / SSIM / MSE: NUS-WIDE 87.36 / 0.979 / 0.26; MS-COCO 71.62 / 0.980 / 0.18; IAPR-TC 67.59 / 0.980 / 0.27 (best SSIM and MSE among all attacks)
V2L benign BA baselines: NUS-WIDE 79.93, MS-COCO 88.07, IAPR-TC 67.62; BadCM BA stays ~equal (79.84 / 87.98 / 67.47)
L2V (Table II) BadCM: MS-COCO ASR 64.96, SBert 0.73, GErr 0.23; IAPR-TC ASR 61.25, SBert 0.73, GErr 1.03 (best SBert, >6% over others)
SPECTRE at 40% poisoning: remaining poison ratio BadCM 45.3% vs BadNets 25.0%
DB-R post-defense ASR: BadCM 72.28% vs BadNets 9.79% (MS-COCO)
Februus post-defense ASR: BadCM 71.22% vs BadNets 20.99%
Fine-Pruning: BadCM ASR >70% at 85% neurons pruned; BadNets drops to ~50% at 80% pruned
ONION (textual) post-defense ASR: BadCM 36.05% vs BadNets 11.16%
Dual-key VQA (Table IX) average BadCM ASR: MCAN 87.28, BAN 82.56, BUTD 83.78, MFB 84.33, MFH 83.90 (vs DKMB ~98.35-98.85, visible)
Ablation (Table VI, MS-COCO): BadCM ASR 71.62 / SSIM 0.980 vs global 71.69 / 0.962, random 69.23 / 0.971, fixed 69.11 / 0.968
Surrogate (Table VII): CLIP V2L 71.62 / L2V 64.96 ASR; ViLT 71.29 / 65.73; R50+LSTM 70.03 / 58.99
Default poisoning rate 5%; textual candidate set N=64, target score 0.7; image invariant area <=30%, text keywords <=40%
baselines_compared: O2BA [19] image-captioning V2L attack (cannot do L2V)
Defenses: SPECTRE [56], DB-R [22], Fine-Pruning [20], Februus [21], ONION [58]
Victim retrieval models: DSCMR [39], ACMR [38], DCMH [37] (VGG-16 / ResNet-50)
Victim VQA models: MCAN, BAN, BUTD, MFB, MFH
